comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the use of array languages like APL and its derivatives for audio synthesis, with contributors sharing resources, technical insights, and suggestions for improving usability. The original author and others discuss implementation details and future plans for the synthesizer project.

comments.author: UncleEntity
comments.text:
> ...and the right-to-left evaluation logic.

The evaluation order doesn't matter as much as you don't really know what kind of function/operator you have at parse time so have to do a bunch of shenanigans to defer that decision until runtime while still keeping it efficient. Kind of fiddly to get right but once it works, it just works.

Claude and me (and a ton of decades old research) pretty much figured out all the complications in the APL parse/eval stack (https://github.com/dan-eicher/AiPL).
comments.analysis: Provides a detailed and accurate explanation of APL evaluation order complexities, referencing decades of research and a relevant GitHub project.

comments.author: jodrellblank
comments.text:
Marshall Lochbaum's APL-derived language BQN has some nods towards audio synthesis too:

https://mlochbaum.github.io/BQN-Musician/synth/index.html
comments.analysis: Shares a relevant example of a related language with audio synthesis capabilities, adding useful context to the discussion.

comments.author: octetta
comments.text:
On the C-side, I'm going to add a UDP listener to the code so I can send k-synth incantations live from Emacs (something I did for my skred program at the suggestion of an Emacs user).
Let me know if anyone wants to know about this when it's usable.
On the desktop app side, I use miniaudio (thank's Macron) so this is portable to the usual suspects.
I also made a single header file cross platform midi library which I have some devious plans for in this space. Stay tuned.
comments.analysis: Describes planned technical improvements and integrations, showing active development and thoughtful design.

comments.author: octetta
comments.text: A warning... if you save a setup to JSON, it naively stores the generated waveforms put in slots and the notebook, so the files can become quiet large. I have a plan to just keep the code behind the waves and regenerate the waveforms at load time.
comments.analysis: Provides a useful caveat about current implementation with a reasonable plan to improve efficiency.

comments.author: octetta
comments.text:
I have a CLI and desktop versions on github (all MIT license)

https://github.com/octetta/k-synth https://github.com/octetta/ksynth-desktop/

it's been a blast to play with... and a great excuse to learn array languages
comments.analysis: Shares project availability and personal experience, contributing positively to the discussion.

comments.author: jdontillman
comments.text:
See also: Stanley Jordan, "APL For Music".

https://dl.acm.org/doi/pdf/10.1145/75144.75174

(!!!)

(Wait, what? That Stanley Jordan?) (Yep.)
comments.analysis: References a scholarly resource linking APL and music, adding depth though the surprise about the author is anecdotal.

comments.author: hmokiguess
comments.text: Pretty cool, one suggestion for the site would be to have templates you can quickly load, copy, edit, and share. Sort of like strudel.cc has!
comments.analysis: Offers a practical usability suggestion that could enhance the user experience.

comments.author: steveBK123
comments.analysis: Expresses positive sentiment but adds no substantive information.

comments.author: jart
comments.text: How do we get it to play Kompressor? https://youtu.be/9tlA0IyKjiI
